title: Eva only for otakus
wikitext:
[]: Eva "only for otakus"?             Quote Post by Born of Lilith » Tue Nov 16, 2010 10:13 pm I recently read a review of Eva which claimed that it only appeals to the otaku community, and that the writer would be confounded if he met a non-otaku fan. I'm pretty sure that isn't an uncommon assumption either. I'm not an otaku myself. I like Ghost in the Shell, Cowboy Bebop, FLCL, Death Note and a few others, but the only anime I'm really in love with is Eva. I haven't seen any anime that is like Eva on a non-superficial level, so I don't see why it'd have a particular appeal to people who go through anime series like money. For me, I'm consistently frustrated with how much less I like other anime. Actually, a friend of mine who has literally watched and really liked hundreds of anime thinks Eva is silly. Am I the only non-otaku around who loves, and is perhaps a bit obsessed with, Evangelion? I hope I'm not beating a dead horse in any way with my commentary and I'd be interested in reading any insight y'all have to bring to the table.

[]:             Quote Post by Joseph the PRPD » Tue Nov 16, 2010 10:16 pm Eva is also for smart people .

[]:             Quote Post by TehDonutKing » Tue Nov 16, 2010 10:41 pm Being an otaku would probably make you like it more, but I'm not much of an otaku either.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Tue Nov 16, 2010 10:43 pm Joseph the PRPD wrote: Eva is also for smart people .

[]: Re: Eva "only for otakus"?             Quote Post by Merridian » Tue Nov 16, 2010 10:59 pm Born of Lilith wrote: I haven't seen any anime that is like Eva on a non-superficial level, so I don't see why it'd have a particular appeal to people who go through anime series like money.

[]:             Quote Post by soul.assassin » Tue Nov 16, 2010 11:28 pm At the height of the TV series, and moreso by the time the last five episodes came in, even salarymen in bars were openly debating Eva's merits over their beer and finger food; the whole country was literally watching Eva, hopped to the eyeballs, from kids, housewives, teens (the very target audience), the aforementioned salarymen, even government officials, wondering where the storyline was leading to... http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?t=10104 Which is why 2.0 came on top on opening day, beating Transformers: Revenge of the Fallen in the Japanese box office.

[]:             Quote Post by The Abhorrent » Tue Nov 16, 2010 11:42 pm NGE transcends the genre a bit and is entertaining to more than it's intended audience, a breakout hit of sorts. My interests in anime are actually rather narrow, and in retrospect I'm more likely to enjoy western animation (predominantly from when I was a kid, current releases tend to be hit-or-miss). However, Evangelion really stands out for a wide variety of reasons. It's intelligent & well-written, and often steps into some very dark territory without being gratuitous about it. It's kinda surprising when you read it's intended for young teens (around the age of the pilots, which does make some sense) when the series is tackles some very adult topics in a mature fashion. Like a lot of well-written shows, Evangelion doesn't require that the viewer be well aquainted with common themes and cliches of the genre (nor obscure referrences) in order to be enjoyed. Being familiar with those (by either having watched many anime series, or just having trekked through TV Tropes a few times) however may make the experience more interesting/enjoyable/whatever. Joseph the PRPD wrote: Eva is also for smart people .

[]:             Quote Post by Born of Lilith » Tue Nov 16, 2010 11:49 pm Something I find interesting is how starkly my experience with Eva contrasts most others': when I first fell in love with Evangelion, I was fairly inept academically and hadn't seen any anime prior aside from Yu-Gi-Oh! and Dragon Ball Z.

[]: Re: Eva "only for otakus"?             Quote Post by soul.assassin » Wed Nov 17, 2010 12:14 am Born of Lilith wrote: I recently read a review of Eva which claimed that it only appeals to the otaku community, and that the writer would be confounded if he met a non-otaku fan.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Nov 17, 2010 12:18 am ITT: several Otaku in denial

[]: Re: Eva "only for otakus"?             Quote Post by Born of Lilith » Wed Nov 17, 2010 12:32 am soul.assassin wrote: Forgot to ask, but where did you read that review?

[]: Re: Eva "only for otakus"?             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Nov 17, 2010 12:46 am Born of Lilith wrote: Hurr hurr. Enjoying a few anime classics doesn't make me an otaku any more than listening to an occasional Kreator or Mayhem song and then listening to stuff like t.A.T.u. and Katy Perry the rest of the time makes me a sold out metalhead. :)

[]:             Quote Post by Jayfive » Wed Nov 17, 2010 9:57 am I don't remember the site's name. If I find the article again, though, I'll let you know.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Wed Nov 17, 2010 11:08 am Neon Genesis Evangelion was only the second TV anime I watched all the way through; the first one being season 1 of Gunslinger Girl . I had only seen a few anime features by Miyazaki by that time, though the first anime feature I ever saw was AKIRA . So within strictly anime, I was not an otaku at the time I saw Neon Genesis Evangelion . I'm not even sure if I am now. BUT... I did get into anime when I was at film school, and only after a friend of mine who already seemed quite knowledgeable in film recommended AKIRA to me. I was only aware of the Yu-Gi-Ohs and Dragon Balls of anime at the time, and really had a certain amount of disgust toward anime myself. Secretly wanting to prove to my friend that pretentious film people like me were not interested in stupid Japanese cartoons, I borrowed AKIRA from the film school's library. (There were plenty of animation student who were otakus. I thought the anime selections in that school were just to appease those guys.) I also borrowed Koyaanisqatsi and Russian Ark with the movie to make sure I got my film pretentiousness in along with that stupid anime. After seeing AKIRA , I was blown away. That movie was amazing. Afterward I started getting into more and more anime, even attending some of the events that my friend held in his anime group he started. Heck, I even help him run his anime group now. Neon Genesis Evangelion may only be for otakus, depending on your definition of "otaku". If you just take it as its literal Japanese translation of "obsessive fan", then yes. I am an "obsessive fan" of film, which is why I got into anime and eventually saw Neon Genesis Evangelion .

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Wed Nov 17, 2010 2:07 pm When first viewing Neon Genesis Evangelion , I was not even a fan of anime. The only one I had really enjoyed at the time was Fullmetal Alchemist . Even after watching Evangelion I was not too interested in the medium; it wasn't until Tengen Toppa Gurren Lagann that I started branching out my interest in different anime, and eventually becoming somewhat of an otaku— though, only in the sense that I watch and discuss anime on a regular basis. At that point, I had already joined this forum and discussed the hell out of the series, thus making me an example of a non-otaku who loved Evangelion.

[]:             Quote Post by InstrumentalityOne » Wed Nov 17, 2010 4:24 pm Define Otaku

[]: Re: Eva "only for otakus"?             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Wed Nov 17, 2010 4:26 pm Born of Lilith wrote: I recently read a review of Eva which claimed...

[]:             Quote Post by MugwumpHasNoLiver » Wed Nov 17, 2010 6:16 pm Well, I liked the anime available to me when I was little. Shit like Pokemon, Dragon Ball Z, Yu-Gi-Oh, etc. But by the time immediately proceeding me watching Eva, I was starting to consciously reject all of it. I recall distinctly thinking, when I was young, that anime was superior in most ways to Western animation, then doing a complete 180. Right before I watched Eva, I was sick of anime, because it seemed like most of it was a series of trite fight scenes with one dimensional characters engaging in ludicrous spectacle in order to keep the attention of it's glazed, indifferent audience. I wanted something with depth, and intrigue, something surreal and freakish. Something bitchin , dude! I actually wrote Eva off as being that kind of childish banality when I first saw it. It was episode two I think, and there was this dumb naked kid doing wacky shit with a penguin, so I was like "The hell with this crap!" There were exceptions, of course. I saw Paranoia Agent before seeing Eva, and thought it was a masterpiece, but I also thought of it as the exception to the rule. Anyway, long story short, I saw episodes 25 and 26 while they were re-running them on adult swim, and realized that I'd made a gross error in judgment. Actually, Eva only deepened my resentment to anime. Suddenly, none of it was good anymore. Well, okay, most of it wasn't good before, but I recognized the possibility that good stuff existed. Afterwards, I didn't feel like anything could live up to Eva, so I completely lost interest in all of it. Basically, I stopped watching it all together for a long time. Recently, I've watched a series or two, now and then, if it strikes my fancy. But I don't really think of any of the anime I watch as being particularly different from any of the other films or TV shows I watch. I really don't have any interest in anime, or otaku culture, and I think that label it's got attached to itself is a really hindrance. That label, that stereotype, got in the way and made me disregard an entire medium as childish and stupid. Now that that's out of the way, I'd just like to say that the subversive elements might mean more to otakus, Eva can be appreciated by anybody who likes an esoteric and complex plot, and well-developed characters. I sincerely doubt that the majority of those people are otakus.

[]:             Quote Post by Born of Lilith » Wed Nov 17, 2010 6:49 pm Jayfive wrote: Its not this one is it? http://www.catsonmars.com/otaku/articles/eva.php Funny thing if it was, because I only found that yesterday from evamonkey.com :)

[]:             Quote Post by TonyTH » Wed Nov 17, 2010 10:31 pm I have a buddy who is quite obsessed with anime. And I've slowly come to know over the years that most of it is just generic garbage with so little substance. Suuure, there's some gems out there. And some of it is amazing. I just watched Baccano after several recommendations, and quite enjoyed it. But hell, before that and Eva(I'm a late watcher) all I was really into was Dragonball. And that's just nostalgia, I just love the stuff. After that though? Didn't care for too much of anything until I watched Code Geass. Geass is amazing to me, so fast paced and interesting. Awesome story and music. But really? Geass kind of killed anime to me. It was so well done and everything just paled in comparison. Similar to the way Eva killed anime for some of you. I have another friend who enjoys storytelling and whatnot just as much as I do. Love movies/games/shows what have you that tell a good story. If it has this quality we usually love it. We also mock the cliches and predictability of some stories. It's all good fun. This friend also enjoyed Eva very much, was harping on me for years to watch it. I didn't know what to think. "Surely it won't be as good as Geass." That came up. I also knew it would be a mindfuck. I did skim through EoE footage online a bit and was quite disturbed by it(New fan rule #1, don't start with EoE!). But I was like "This guy's never steered me wrong, lets give it a shot." At first I thought it was fairly generic...although a bit complex. I mean, the beginning isn't too crazy. It's pretty straightforward at that too. I was thinking "Oh, it's just because it's a classic. That's why it's so beloved." Unbeknown to me 100 images are passing by with multiple meanings and all that crazy stuff Anno has thrown in. I kept watching. Certainly not bored. Enjoying it. Then I watch as things get really confusing and complex. Mindfucks start to occur and horrifying gore ensues. The Eva-04 scene screwed with me a bit, but my God...the Zeruel scene was really disturbing on the first few watches. Eva-01 was really scary with it's movements and noises. The show then broke down boundaries, begins to break you down mentally and then ends... Then comes EoE. I was honestly ready for anything at that point, and the show had broken me down slowly to be prepared for the craziness that ensued. What I saw was a masterpiece of a movie, and one of the greatest examples of film of all time. And then I truly saw what all the fuss was about. I was entranced and obsessed at that point. It was just an amazing watch. And I think a point to make is that I didn't even really know what was going on fully, and I still found it deep and compelling. It makes you WANT to research the series to find it's secrets. But even if you don't you KNOW you just watched something amazing and unforgettable. You can, as Anno intended, come to your own conclusions. And you can still admire the imagery/direction/music/deep story for what it is without doing the research. It's just enjoyable on so many levels. And now I have trouble finding myself wanted to re-watch Geass. Which is a damn good story on it's own merit. But there's just something about Eva...and it's honestly become my favorite story I've ever had the pleasure of watching. It set a standard for compelling/smart story telling that I will never forget. ...My life story aside, the answer is "No, not just for otakus" ; P
